ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for chilaquiles; mapped to corn tortilla-based dish as the closest defensible FoodOn ancestor. Chilaquiles is a Mexican dish of fried corn tortilla chips simmered in salsa with eggs, chicken, cheese, and sour cream.
ontology.foodex2_description: Corn tortilla dish — mixed preparation with sauce, eggs, and cheese
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (tortilla-based dish) used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. No exact chilaquiles entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for chilaquiles identified in the clinical terminology as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of chilaquiles is approximately 250 g (one plate), consisting of a generous portion of fried corn tortilla chips simmered in red or green salsa, topped with eggs or shredded chicken, crumbled cheese, sour cream, and sliced avocado.

nutrient_source.title: Chilaquiles — HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database entry G00010812
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record G00010812 for Chilaquiles. All per-100g macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are sourced from this record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB record are set to -1 and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Corn tortilla, GI = 52 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for chilaquiles exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 52 based on corn tortilla (GI = 52, low category) from the International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Chilaquiles are made primarily from corn tortilla chips simmered in salsa; the corn tortilla base dominates the carbohydrate fraction. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 14.9 × 2.5) / 100 = 52 × 37.25 / 100 = 19.4 GL units for a 250 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 366 mg/100 g with insufficient potassium offset (164 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit chilaquiles to small portions and request reduced-sodium salsa, minimal added salt, and no extra cheese or sour cream, as sodium at 366 mg/100 g is borderline-high and potassium at 164 mg/100 g is insufficient to offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (14.9 g/100 g) with low GI corn tortilla base, low sugar (1.12 g/100 g), and protein (10.63 g/100 g) moderating glycemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes may consume chilaquiles in moderate portions, as the corn tortilla base has a low GI (estimated 52) and protein content helps blunt glycemic response, but should request baked rather than fried tortilla chips and limit sour cream and cheese to reduce saturated fat that may worsen insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 10.67 g/100 g from fried tortilla chips, cheese, and sour cream; cholesterol 141 mg/100 g; sodium 366 mg/100 g; avocado provides beneficial MUFA
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume chilaquiles sparingly due to elevated cholesterol (141 mg/100 g), high sodium (366 mg/100 g), and fat from fried preparation and dairy toppings; requesting baked tortilla chips, reduced cheese and sour cream, and extra avocado can improve the cardiovascular profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fried preparation generating AGEs; nightshade ingredients (tomato, chili, onion); saturated fat from cheese and sour cream
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit chilaquiles due to the pro-inflammatory combination of AGEs from deep-fried tortilla chips, nightshade vegetables (tomato, chili, onion), and saturated fat from cheese and sour cream; substituting baked tortilla chips and increasing avocado portion may partially mitigate the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilaquiles is a traditional Mexican breakfast and brunch dish consisting of fried corn tortilla chips (totopos) that are simmered in red (rojo) or green (verde) salsa until they soften slightly while retaining some texture. The dish is typically topped with shredded chicken or fried eggs, crumbled queso fresco or cotija cheese, sour cream (crema), sliced avocado, and fresh onion rings. Chilaquiles originated in Mexico and are widely consumed as a morning meal or hangover remedy. The corn tortilla base provides a moderate glycemic index (estimated GI 52, low category) and contributes dietary fibre (2.1 g/100 g). The dish is protein-rich at 10.63 g/100 g from eggs and chicken, and contains 10.67 g/100 g total fat from fried chips, cheese, and sour cream. Sodium is elevated at 366 mg/100 g, making portion control important for hypertensive individuals. Cholesterol is notable at 141 mg/100 g. The avocado topping contributes monounsaturated fatty acids with anti-inflammatory benefit. Clinically, chilaquiles are moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to the low-GI corn base and protein content, high concern for hypertension due to sodium load, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to fat and cholesterol content. The dish is not suitable for nightshade-sensitive rheumatoid arthritis patients. Energy density is 195 kcal/100 g. A standard serving is approximately 250 g (one plate), providing approximately 488 kcal.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and secondary nutrients sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo record G00010812 (confidence 0.95). GI estimated from the closest approved analogous food (corn tortilla, GI 52) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed source. Fatty acid breakdown (saturated, monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat) not reported in the HPB source and set to -1 with documentation.
notes: Fields set to -1 due to unavailability in the HPB source record G00010812: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Detailed fatty acid breakdown and vitamin profile for this mixed dish preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. The input health_suitability field referenced trans_fat at 0.1 g/100 g; this value is not confirmed in the HPB source record and has been set to -1 pending verification. Glycemic index is an analogous estimate based on corn tortilla (GI 52) from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care); no direct GI measurement for chilaquiles exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving (250 g): GL = 52 × (14.9 × 2.5) / 100 = 52 × 37.25 / 100 = 19.4. Chilaquiles is classified as a Mexican dish; multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese cuisines do not apply. No Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese local names are applicable for this Mexican dish. The input record classified primary_cuisine as 'Western' and ethnic_tags as ['Western']; this has been corrected to 'Mexican' based on the dish's established culinary origin. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry for chilaquiles exists as of 2024.
